title: California Housing Snapshot
content:
# California Housing Snapshot

## Summary
This starter knowledge document captures official California housing context that can support retrieval-augmented answers about the statewide market environment.

## Key Facts
- California's population estimate was 39,355,309 as of July 1, 2025.
- California had 14,877,904 housing units as of July 1, 2024.
- The owner-occupied housing unit rate was 55.9% in 2020-2024.
- The median value of owner-occupied housing units was $734,700 in 2020-2024.
- Median selected monthly owner costs with a mortgage were $2,946 in 2020-2024.
- Median selected monthly own

title: California Housing Snapshot
content:
2020-2024.
- Median selected monthly owner costs with a mortgage were $2,946 in 2020-2024.
- Median selected monthly owner costs without a mortgage were $829 in 2020-2024.
- Median gross rent was $2,036 in 2020-2024.
- Building permits totaled 101,545 in 2024.
- California averaged 2.84 persons per household in 2020-2024.

## Advisory Use
- Use this document when answering broad statewide affordability, ownership-cost, rent, or housing-supply questions.
- This is statewide context and should not be treated as a city-level comp set.

## Sources
- https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/california
- h

title: California Population and Housing Estimates
content:
# California Population and Housing Estimates

## Summary
This document summarizes official California state demographic estimate sources that can help explain housing demand pressure, population shifts, and broad supply context.

## Key Facts
- The California Department of Finance publishes January population and housing estimates for cities, counties, and the state.
- The Department of Finance provides E-1 annual population and housing estimates with annual percent change.
- The Department of Finance also provides E-5 population and housing estimates for cities, counties, and the state acros

title: California Population and Housing Estimates
content:
- The Department of Finance also provides E-5 population and housing estimates for cities, counties, and the state across recent years.
- These sources are useful for tracking whether local housing demand may be supported by population and housing-stock changes over time.

## Advisory Use
- Use this document when an answer needs official California estimates rather than model-internal feature importance alone.
- These estimates are useful for contextual explanations about demand, supply pressure, and demographic trend direction.

## Sources
- https://dof.ca.gov/Forecasting/Demographics/Estimat

title: California Population and Housing Estimates
content:
and, supply pressure, and demographic trend direction.

## Sources
- https://dof.ca.gov/Forecasting/Demographics/Estimates/

title: California Population Projections
content:
# California Population Projections

## Summary
This document summarizes official California population projection resources that can support long-range housing demand commentary.

## Key Facts
- The California Department of Finance Demographic Research Unit publishes state and county population projections through 2070.
- The published baseline includes total population projections, age breakdowns, sex breakdowns, and county projections.
- These projections are useful for long-horizon advisory responses, such as where sustained demand may matter more than a single-year price estimate.

## Adv

title: California Population Projections
content:
ng-horizon advisory responses, such as where sustained demand may matter more than a single-year price estimate.

## Advisory Use
- Use this document when questions ask about future housing pressure, long-term demand, or population-driven planning context.
- Projection outputs should be treated as planning context, not direct transaction-price evidence.

## Sources
- https://dof.ca.gov/Forecasting/Demographics/projections/

title: Demo Listing Inventory Snapshot
content:
# Demo Listing Inventory Snapshot

## Summary
This document summarizes the current seeded property-listings inventory used by the local search and recommendation flows. It is a demo inventory snapshot, not an official market dataset.

## Key Facts
- The current seed inventory includes listings across San Francisco, San Jose, Oakland, San Diego, and Sacramento.
- Oakland demo listings currently span roughly the low `$600k` range for starter condos to about `$1.49M` for larger family houses.
- Sacramento remains the lowest-priced city in the current demo inventory, with entry-level apartments an

title: Demo Listing Inventory Snapshot
content:
family houses.
- Sacramento remains the lowest-priced city in the current demo inventory, with entry-level apartments and condos below many Bay Area alternatives.
- San Francisco and San Jose listings skew higher overall, especially for family-sized units with 3 or more bedrooms.
- Condos and apartments in the seeded inventory usually represent lower entry-price options than detached houses in the same city.

## City Notes
### Oakland
- Demo Oakland inventory includes urban condos, townhouses, and larger houses.
- Listings mentioning BART or stronger commute access should be preferred for tran

title: Demo Listing Inventory Snapshot
content:
ondos, townhouses, and larger houses.
- Listings mentioning BART or stronger commute access should be preferred for transit-oriented searches.
- Starter-level Oakland options exist in the condo segment, while larger family-oriented houses sit much higher in price.

### San Jose
- Demo San Jose inventory mixes employer-adjacent apartments with more spacious family houses.
- North San Jose and Downtown listings are better suited for entry or commuter-oriented searches than Almaden or Evergreen family houses.

### San Francisco
- Demo San Francisco inventory remains expensive even for smaller con

title: Demo Listing Inventory Snapshot
content:
den or Evergreen family houses.

### San Francisco
- Demo San Francisco inventory remains expensive even for smaller condos.
- Transit- and walkability-oriented searches are more likely to surface apartment or condo products than detached houses.

### San Diego
- Demo San Diego inventory shows a split between coastal premium pricing and more moderate urban or inland options.
- Family-oriented house searches tend to surface Clairemont or North Park style listings before coastal luxury products.

### Sacramento
- Demo Sacramento inventory offers the strongest affordability within the current see

title: Demo Listing Inventory Snapshot
content:
l luxury products.

### Sacramento
- Demo Sacramento inventory offers the strongest affordability within the current seed set.
- Buyers with tighter budgets are more likely to find matching options in Midtown, Downtown, Natomas, Curtis Park, or East Sacramento than in Bay Area cities.

## Advisory Use
- Use this document when a query needs a city-level explanation of why some search results are more or less affordable in the demo inventory.
- Do not present this document as official market evidence. It is internal demo context derived from the seeded listing set.

title: Demo Locality Preference Notes
content:
# Demo Locality Preference Notes

## Summary
This document provides demo locality-level notes based on the seeded listing inventory. These notes are intended to help the recommendation and advisory layers explain search tradeoffs more specifically than statewide California context.

## Locality Highlights
### Oakland
- **Lake Merritt**: better fit for buyers who want a balanced city lifestyle with commute access and urban amenities.
- **Temescal**: stronger fit for buyers who want a neighborhood retail feel and townhouse-style living.
- **Rockridge**: better fit for higher-budget family or own

title: Demo Locality Preference Notes
content:
want a neighborhood retail feel and townhouse-style living.
- **Rockridge**: better fit for higher-budget family or owner-occupier searches, especially when BART access matters.
- **Uptown**: better for lower-entry city buyers than large-family buyers.
- **Jack London Square**: better for transit-connected urban buyers than for family-size space seekers.
- **Montclair**: better for larger-home and family-oriented searches than for budget-constrained buyers.

### San Jose
- **North San Jose**: better for commuter-oriented or employer-adjacent searches.
- **Downtown San Jose**: stronger for urb

title: Demo Locality Preference Notes
content:
*North San Jose**: better for commuter-oriented or employer-adjacent searches.
- **Downtown San Jose**: stronger for urban, entry-level, or transit-friendly searches.
- **Willow Glen**: balanced owner-occupier location for townhouse buyers.
- **Almaden Valley** and **Evergreen**: stronger fit for larger-family space than for budget or starter searches.

### San Francisco
- **SoMa** and **Mission District**: stronger fit for urban, commute, or investment-oriented searches.
- **Sunset District**, **Inner Sunset**, and **Noe Valley**: better fit for buyers who prioritize neighborhood livability o

title: Demo Locality Preference Notes
content:
*Sunset District**, **Inner Sunset**, and **Noe Valley**: better fit for buyers who prioritize neighborhood livability over lowest entry price.
- **Richmond District**: more suitable for starter condo positioning than premium family-house searches.

### San Diego
- **Mission Valley** and **Hillcrest**: stronger fit for transit, central access, or smaller-unit searches.
- **North Park** and **Clairemont**: more balanced for owner-occupier and family-style searches.
- **La Jolla**: premium lifestyle positioning with less budget flexibility.
- **Little Italy**: better fit for urban/investment-sty

title: Demo Locality Preference Notes
content:
a**: premium lifestyle positioning with less budget flexibility.
- **Little Italy**: better fit for urban/investment-style searches than larger-family needs.

### Sacramento
- **Midtown** and **Downtown**: stronger for lower-entry urban searches.
- **Natomas**: practical value-oriented townhouse option.
- **East Sacramento**, **Land Park**, and **Curtis Park**: stronger fit for owner-occupier and family-style searches.

## Advisory Use
- Use this document when explaining why one returned listing may fit a lifestyle or search intent better than another.
- These are demo preference notes derived

title: Demo Locality Preference Notes
content:
one returned listing may fit a lifestyle or search intent better than another.
- These are demo preference notes derived from the seed inventory and listing descriptions, not external market research.
